content.full_text:
Law Offices of
LAWRENCE A. HAUN
Steven and One-half South Washington Street
Post Office Box 1056
Sonora, California 95370
Telephone (209) 532-1958    Telecopier (209) 532-1963

Lawrence A. Haun
Roger A. Brown

February 12, 1991


Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

RE: Request for Advice

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     This is to request specific advice concerning my clients'
duties under the Political Reform Act of 1974, as amended, ("The
Act") pursuant to Government Code section 83114(b).  My clients
are BKK Corporation, Greenfield Environmental and their
affiliated entities and employees.

QUESTIONS PRESENTED

     1.  Who is the lobbyist employer to be identified on Form
635 where an "in-house" employee of two affiliated companies is a
registered lobbyist and a portion of her salary is paid by each
affiliated entity?

     2.  Where a lobbyist is an employee of two companies with a
portion of her salary paid by each, and she lobbies only for
these two companies, is she a lobbying firm or an employee of a
lobbyist employer for registration and reporting purposes?

FACTS

     Kathy Fletcher is a new lobbyist and an in-house employee of
both Greenfield Environmental and BKK Corporation.  She is paid a
salary and has deductions taken out for payroll taxes.  Ms.
Fletcher is eligible for the normal benefits accorded to
employees.  She has all the customary earmarks of an employee.
The hours, conditions and manner of accomplishing her tasks are
set by her employer.  Ms. Fletcher does not operate her own
business, has filed no fictitious business name statement and
works out of the Greenfield offices in Sacramento.  Ms. Fletcher
is an employee, not an independent contractor.

     Ms. Fletcher is paid by payroll check drawn on the payroll
account of Greenfield Environmental.  However, an affiliated
entity, BKK Corporation, reimburses Greenfield for a portion of

Scott Hallabrin
February 12, 1991
Page 2

Ms. Fletcher's salary. The companies each reimburse Ms. Fletcher
for her expenses. In turn, Ms. Fletcher is registered to lobby
on behalf of both BKK Corporation and Greenfield Environmental.

BKK Corporation and Greenfield Environmental are affiliated
entities within the meaning of 2 California Code of Regulations
section 18428(b). The companies have common shareholders,
directors and managers and file Major Donor Campaign Statements
aggregating their contributions. The lobbying efforts of both
companies are coordinated. Kathy Fletcher works at the direction
of the common management. Her employment agreement states, among
other things, that she will:

Monitor all state legislative and
administrative agency proposed laws and
regulations affecting the business activities
of Greenfield and BKK.

Represent Greenfield, and if requested, BKK
Corporation, before the State Legislature and
State administrative agencies

When it came time for BKK and Greenfield to register with
the Secretary of State as lobbyist employers, the FPPC technical
assistance staff advised Ms. Fletcher and me that she should
register as a lobbying firm and her employers need not register
at all, but should continue filing Form 635's as a lobbyist
employer. We have followed this advice but wish to receive
formal written advice because we are confused about the authority
for the advice we were given.

DISCUSSION

A "Lobbyist Employer" is defined in Government Code section
82039.5 to mean, among other things, "any person other than a
lobbying firm who:

(a) Employs one or more lobbyists for
economic consideration....
(b) Contracts for the services of a lobbying
firm for economic consideration....(emphasis
added.)

In contrast, a "Lobbying Firm" is defined in Government Code
section 82038.5 to mean:

any business entity, including an individual
contract lobbyist, which meets either of the
following criteria:

Scott Hallabrin
February 12, 1991
Page 3

(1) The business entity receives or becomes
entitled to receive any compensation...for
the purpose of influencing legislative or
administrative action on behalf of any other
person, and any partner, owner, officer, or
employee of the business entity is a
lobbyist.

(2) The business entity receives or becomes
entitled to receive any compensation...to
communicate directly with any elective state
official, agency official, or legislative
official for the purpose of influencing
legislative or administrative action on
behalf of any other person, if a substantial
or regular portion of the activities for
which the business entity receives
compensation is for the purpose of
influencing legislative or administrative
action.(Emphasis added.)

Significantly, only business entities may be lobbying firms
as the term is defined in the Act. A "Business Entity" is
defined to be,

any organization or enterprise operated for
profit, including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership, firm, business
trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation
or association. (Government Code section
82005.)

Kathy Fletcher is an employee, not a business entity. The
clear meaning of the statute can lead to only one conclusion,
that an employee of a lobbying firm cannot be a lobbying
firm. However clear the statute, there are additional reasons
why Kathy Fletcher cannot be a lobbying firm.

Commission Regulation 18238.5 further defines a lobbying
firm and continues the requirement that a lobbying firm be a
business entity with a partner, owner, officer, or employee of
the business entity who engages in direct communication. Thus,
both the statute and the regulation limit the definition of
lobbying firm to certain "business entities" as defined. The
very concept of Lobbying firm suggests something other than an
individual employee. But, there is more.

The FPPC Information Manual On Lobbying Disclosure
Provisions Of The Political Reform Act (1988 Ed.,"The Manual")
describes what is a lobbying firm. There are a number of

Scott Hallabrin
February 12, 1991
Page 4

examples to illustrate the definition. The only example of an
employee concerns an employee of a lobbying firm who receives
payments directly from each of two different lobbyist employers.
In this situation, the lobbyist is an "individual contract
lobbyist" who is required to register as a lobbying firm.

The example is significantly different from Kathy Fletcher's
situation in at least two important respects. First, Ms.
Fletcher is not an employee of a lobbying firm. She is an
employee of a lobbyist employer. Second, the FPPC has already
advised us that DBK Corporation and Greenfield are a single
lobbyist employer who must file as such on a single Form 635.
Thus, Ms. Fletcher's situation differs from the example for the
additional reason that she receives payments directly from a
single lobbyist employer.

Another example in the Manual is helpful for what it
implies. The fourth example on page 3 of the Manual describes
"Barbara Brown" (no relation) as a lobbying firm where she has a
contract with a corporation but,

"The corporation incurs no payroll taxes for
Brown and does not provide her with health,
retirement or other benefits. Even though
Brown has no other clients or employers,
because she is not an employee of the
corporation, she is an "individual contract
lobbyist" and must register as a lobbying
firm and file periodic reports."(emphasis
added.)

Kathy Fletcher is an employee of the corporation which does
incur payroll taxes and does offer her employee benefits.
Therefore, by necessary implication, Kathy Fletcher is not an
"individual contract lobbyist" and should not be required to
register as a lobbying firm.

Finally, the forms devised by the FPPC clearly show the
Commission never intended for employees to register and report as
lobbying firms. There is no place on Form 625 (Report of
Lobbying Firm) to disclose such things as payroll tax, health or
retirement benefits or other normal employee benefits. These are
payments which are normally made only to employees, not
independent contractors. While the form has no place for wages,
salary or benefits, it does capture such normal independent
contractor payments as "fees and retainers."

If an employee is required to report as a lobbying firm,
significant problems emerge. For example, is the employee
required to report gross or net salary on Form 625? If payroll

Scott Hallabrin
February 12, 1991
Page 5

taxes and benefits are payments received in connection with
lobbying activity, where are they to be reported? Since Kathy
Fletcher is not a firm or a business entity, who are the
"partners, owners, officers or employees" of the lobbying firm
who are to be identified on Part I of Form 625?

CONCLUSION

The Political Reform Act, the Regulations promulgated by the
Fair Political Practices Commission, the Manual issued by the
FPPC and the forms are in agreement that a lobbying firm cannot
be an individual employee of a lobbyist employer. The lobbying
firm must be a "business entity", as defined, or an individual
contract lobbyist. Since Kathy Fletcher is neither a business
entity nor an individual contract lobbyist, she should not be
required to register as a lobbying firm.

We respectfully ask the Commission to render formal advice
consistent with this analysis. The effect of this advice would
be to permit Kathy Fletcher to file periodic lobbyist reports and
to require BKK Corporation and Greenfield Environmental to
register as a lobbyist employer. Kathy Fletcher would withdraw
her prior registration as a lobbying firm. BKK and Greenfield
would continue to file periodic Form 635's.

The results of the advice we seek are consistent with the
spirit, intent and literal meaning of the Political Reform Act,
the Regulations, the Forms and the Manual. No significant
information will be lost, but Ms. Fletcher will be saved the
useless task of filing redundant reports as both a lobbyist and a
lobbying firm.

Very truly yours,

Roger A. Brown

RAB:s|46/004

cc: Clients

sections.question:
1. Who is the lobbyist employer to be identified on Form 635 where an "in-house" employee of two affiliated companies is a registered lobbyist and a portion of her salary is paid by each affiliated entity?

2. Where a lobbyist is an employee of two companies with a portion of her salary paid by each, and she lobbies only for these two companies, is she a lobbying firm or an employee of a lobbyist employer for registration and reporting purposes?

sections.question_synthetic: The requestor seeks FPPC advice on whether Kathy Fletcher, an in-house employee of two affiliated companies (BKK Corporation and Greenfield Environmental) who is paid partially by each entity and registered to lobby for both, should be classified as a lobbying firm or as an employee of a lobbyist employer for registration purposes.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an incoming REQUEST for advice, not an FPPC response letter. The requestor provides extensive discussion and argument in addition to questions, seeking formal written confirmation of prior informal technical assistance advice. Document is dated February 12, 1991 (though header shows 1997). No formal FPPC conclusion/response is present in this document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Who is the lobbyist employer to be identified on Form 635 where an "in-house" employee of two affiliated companies is a registered lobbyist and a portion of her salary is paid by each affiliated entity?

2. Where a lobbyist is an employee of two companies with a portion of her salary paid by each, and she lobbies only for these two companies, is she a lobbying firm or an employee of a lobbyist employer for registration and reporting purposes?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from attorney Roser A. Brown seeking formal FPPC advice regarding the proper registration and reporting classification for Kathy Fletcher, an in-house lobbyist employee of two affiliated entities. The letter argues that Fletcher should register as a lobbyist employee rather than as a lobbying firm, supported by statutory analysis, FPPC regulations, manuals, and form design.
